title: New Crypto Scams to Watch Out For in 2026
transcript: Since  our  last  video  on  crypto  scams,   things  have  arguably  gotten  a  lot  worse.   In  2024  alone,  scammers  stole  at  least   9.9  billion  in  crypto,  and  scammers  are   only  getting  smarter  and  more  creative   every  year.  And  danger  isn't  always   obvious  anymore.  So,  this  video  is  an   updated  breakdown  of  the  latest  crypto   scams  and  how  you  can  avoid  becoming  a   victim.   Let's  start  with  the  time  old  social   engineering.  Scammers  have  always   targeted  victims  through  platforms  and   people  that  they  already  trust.  But  it's   only  getting  scarily  more  believable.  A   few  cases  surfaced  where  scammers  tried   to  get  you  to  install  malware  onto  your   device  via  a  fake  Zoom  link  through  a   compromised  account.  They  impersonate  a   co-orker,  a  friend,  or  someone  that   you've  talked  to  before  on  Telegram,  and   they  say  that  they  want  to  catch  up  or   discuss  a  project,  and  then  send  you   what  looks  like  a  Zoom  link.  And  that   fake  link  then  takes  you  to  a  clone  site   that  looks  identical  to  the  real  thing.   Then  once  you  join  the  call,  you'll   likely  see  familiar  faces,  but  they're   not  real.  Just  [music]   convincing  enough  deep  fakes.  Wow,  that   looks  a  lot  like  Claire.  I  can't.  It's  a   little  bit  creepy.  And  now  that  you're   convinced  that  you're  in  a  real  Zoom   call  with  people  that  you  know,  the  real   bait  appears  in  the  form  of  a  small   technical  issue.  They'll  say  that  they   can't  hear  you  or  that  your  Zoom  version   needs  a  quick  update.  And  since  the  site   is  controlled  by  the  attacker,  a  popup   to  install  that  update  automatically   appears.  Now,  if  you  click  it,  that  is   all  it  takes  to  install  malware  that   gives  them  access  to  your  device,   passwords,  and  crypto  wallets.  This  kind   of  attack  can  take  many  forms.  So,  make   sure  to  never  install  or  run  anything   you  didn't  get  through  official   channels.  Stay  skeptical  and  verify   contacts  on  another  platform  to  make   sure  that  it's  really  them.  But  these   attackers  aren't  just  exploiting  people   that  you  trust.  Even  sites  you  trust  can   be  turned  against  you.  In  June  2025,   Coin  Market  Cap  displayed  a  fake  verify   your  wallet  popup  on  its  website.  Users   who  clicked  it  unknowingly  gave  hackers   permission  to  drain  their  wallets.   Attackers  pulled  this  off  by  exploiting   a  vulnerability  that  made  the  fake  popup   appear  as  if  it  was  coming  from  Coin   Market  Cap  itself.  A  few  days  later,   Cointelegraph  was  hit  by  a  similar   attack  and  briefly  showed  a  fake  airdrop   banner  to  visitors.  These  kinds  of   attacks  that  take  advantage  of  your   trust  in  reputable  platforms  are  not   new.  Attackers  have  in  the  past  managed   to  compromise  a  shared  toolkit  used  by   multiple  crypto  apps  to  handle  wallet   connections.  Suddenly,  any  app  that  used   that  tool  would  unknowingly  run  a  wallet   draining  script  the  moment  a  user   connected.  Then  there  is  an  even  scarier   attack  called  ether  hiding  where  hackers   use  the  blockchain  itself  to  hide  and   deliver  malware.  It  usually  begins  with   social  engineering  where  the  attacker   lures  a  developer  through  a  fake   recruitment  process  which  includes  a   technical  assessment  that  asks  them  to   download  or  run  malicious  code  from  a   normal  source  like  GitHub.  Once  that   code  runs,  it  compromises  the  developer   system  and  lets  the  attacker  slip  their   own  code  into  the  websites  or  projects   that  the  developer  manages.  Then  when   someone  visits  that  site,  the  hidden   script  quietly  reaches  out  to  a   blockchain  network  like  Ethereum  or  BSC   and  pulls  a  small  chunk  of  data  that's   actually  malware  stored  on  chain.  The   malware  runs  right  inside  the  visitor's   browser,  stealing  passwords,  draining   wallets,  or  even  showing  fake  login   pages.  And  because  it's  coming  directly   from  the  blockchain  instead  of  a  normal   server,  there's  no  simple  way  to  take  it   down.  So,  one  thing  that  you  could  do  to   minimize  risk  is  to  use  separate  browser   profiles  or  even  a  dedicated  browser  or   device  just  for  crypto  with  no  logged  in   sessions  or  extensions  that  could  be   hijacked.  Also,  use  wallets  that  warn   you  about  risky  permissions.  And  also   keep  an  eye  on  project  updates  so  that   you'll  know  quickly  if  a  site  that  you   use  has  been  compromised.  And  then   fishing  has  also  evolved,  making  them   harder  to  spot  than  ever.  On  mobile,   some  fishing  sites  can  still  fake  the   browser's  address  bar.  It's  an  old   trick,  but  it  still  works  to  make  fake   sites  look  real.  In  this  demo,  for   example,  you'll  see  a  page  that  looks   like  HSBC's  official  site.  It  even  shows   the  green  padlock  and  www.hsbc.com   in  the  bar.  The  real  site  is  still   visible  at  the  very  top,  but  the  fake   bar,  it  looks  convincing  enough  that   some  people  just  wouldn't  notice  that   they're  on  a  fishing  page.  And  then   there's  email  fishing,  except  this  time   the  scam  emails  actually  come  from  what   looks  like  a  trusted  source.  Hackers   have  managed  to  send  emails  that  appear   to  come  directly  from  Google  or   Microsoft.  And  if  you  take  the  bait,   you'll  end  up  unknowingly  sharing   sensitive  information  with  the  scammer.   Or  in  some  cases,  they'll  try  to  get  you   to  install  malware  on  your  device.  Now,   since  these  fishing  emails  can  slip  past   spam  filters  and  look  pretty  legitimate,   don't  trust  an  email  just  because  of   where  it  says  that  it's  from.  Always   check  the  links  before  clicking.  And  if   you  get  a  critical  alert  to  go  straight   to  the  official  website  or  app  to  verify   it  yourself.  And  sometimes  attackers   don't  even  need  to  trick  you  into   clicking  anything.  They'll  just  wait  for   you  to  install  the  wrong  app.  [music]  In   June  this  year,  researchers  uncovered  a   spyware  that  had  slipped  into  both  the   Apple  App  Store  and  Google  Play  Store.   And  its  goal  was  to  simply  steal  seed   phrases  and  private  keys  from  your   phone.  It  hid  inside  apps  like  a  crypto   portfolio  tracker  on  iOS  or  a  messaging   app  with  crypto  features  on  Android,   each  with  thousands  of  downloads.  But   once  it  was  installed,  the  malware   quietly  scanned  your  photo  gallery  and   files  for  anything  that  looked  like  a   seed  phrase  or  wallet  credentials.  And   if  it  spotted  something  resembling  a   seed  phrase  or  private  key,  it  flagged   that  image  and  uploaded  it  to  the   attacker's  server.  So  the  takeaway  here   is  simple.  Never  save  your  seed  phrase   or  private  key  in  any  digital  form  on   your  phone.  Stick  to  trusted  apps  and   avoid  downloading  random  APKs.  And   finally,  don't  grant  [music]  unnecessary   permissions  to  apps.  So,  scammers  have   also  found  a  way  to  cash  in  on  the  gold   rush  created  by  [music]  memecoins.  One   of  the  tricks  going  around  is  the  fake   MEV  trading  bot.  You've  probably  seen   those  YouTube  videos  that  promise  easy   money.   >> [music]   >> Just  deploy  this  bot,  send  a  little  ETH,   and  let  it  automatically  find  arbitrage   opportunities  for  you.  [music]  Except   the  moment  that  you  do,  your  ETH  is   gone.  [music]  The  contract  is  built  to   drain  your  wallet  and  send  the  money   straight  [music]  to  the  scammer's   address.  In  one  case,  a  single  address   pulled  in  over  $900,000   from  people  [music]  doing  exactly  this.   And  then  AI,  it  has  only  made  things   worse.  The  people  in  these  videos  aren't   even  real.  They're  deep  fake  avatars   working  around  the  clock  selling  the   same  get-rich  story  over  and  over.  So,   as  a  rule,  never  deploy  code  that  you   don't  understand.  Especially  not  just   because  a  random  video  told  you  to.   [music]   If  it  sounds  too  good  to  be  true,  it   probably  is.  So,  with  that,  you're  all   caught  up  on  some  of  the  more   sophisticated  scams  [music]  that   attackers  are  pulling  off  today.  The   reality  is  crypto  scams  are  going   [music]  to  continue  evolving.  And  no   matter  how  careful  you  think  you  are,   all  it  takes  is  one  moment  of  trust  in   the  wrong  link,  the  [music]  wrong   update,  or  the  wrong  app.  So,  slow  down,   doublech  checkck  everything,  [music]  and   question  anything  that  feels  too  urgent   or  too  good  to  be  true.  So  hopefully   this  video  helped  you  out  and  drop  a   comment  if  you've  come  across  any   [music]  of  these  scams.  Your  experience   might  help  someone  else  avoid  becoming   the  next  victim.   [music]
summary_hu: A 2026-ra vonatkozó legújabb kriptovaluta csalásokat elemezve megállapítható, hogy a csalók egyre innovatívabb módszerekkel operálnak. A társadalmi manipuláció, mint például a hamis Zoom linkek küldése, a legnagyobb veszélyforrások közé tartozik, ahol a támadók ismerősöket utánoznak, hogy kártékony programokat telepítsenek. Weboldalak, amelyekben megbízunk, mint a Coin Market Cap, szintén ellenséges támadások célpontjai lehetnek, és a biztonsági rések kihasználása révén jogosulatlan hozzáféréseket nyújtanak a hacker-támadók számára. Emellett a kriptovaluta alkalmazásokkal kapcsolatban egyre inkább elterjedt a malware rejtése a blokkláncon, amely komoly biztonsági kockázatokat jelent. Az elején törekedni kell a gyanakvásra, a megerősítésre és a megbízható források használatára, így elkerülhetjük a csalásokat.
summary_en: The analysis of the latest crypto scams for 2026 reveals that scammers are utilizing increasingly innovative methods. Social engineering tactics, such as sending fake Zoom links, represent a significant danger, where attackers impersonate acquaintances to install malicious software. Trusted websites like Coin Market Cap can also be targets of hostile attacks, inadvertently allowing unauthorized access due to exploited vulnerabilities. Furthermore, malware hiding within the blockchain has become more prevalent, posing serious security risks. Initially, maintaining skepticism, verification, and using trustworthy sources is essential to avoid falling victim to these scams.
key_points_hu: A társadalmi manipuláció veszélyes eszköz a csalók kezében.
Weboldalak és alkalmazások is biztonsági rések célpontjai.
A kriptovaluta blokkláncon rejlő malware egyre elterjedtebb.
key_points_en: Social engineering is a dangerous tool for scammers.
Websites and applications are also targets of security vulnerabilities.
Malware hidden within the blockchain is increasingly prevalent.
